history: YOLO/MediaPipe hand keypoint detection → Rep counting + form-feedback for hand rehabilitation exercises with real-time overlay
description: The student builds a real-time computer vision application using a pre-trained hand keypoint model to track finger and wrist joints from webcam video and provide live feedback on hand rehabilitation exercises. The core mechanism computes finger extension or grip angles frame-by-frame and fires a rep counter when the joint angle crosses a threshold, with a second form-feedback mode flagging incomplete range of motion or asymmetric finger movement. Deliverable: live demo with hand keypoint overlay, rep count, and form warnings; reflection on failure cases (occluded fingers, skin tone variance) and medical guidance risk.
consultant_notes: Hand keypoint models (YOLOv8-pose or MediaPipe) provide pre-trained weights that reduce training burden; rep-counting from joint angles is tractable in 30 days. Main risk is the form-feedback scenario requiring a reference pose definition. Clear user need in hand rehabilitation and strong demo potential.
annotator_notes: Exhibits both theoretical and engineering problem solving

history: Computer vision model trained on labeled pothole dataset → Severity classification (minor/moderate/severe) with bounding box + severity label demo
description: The student builds a pothole detection and severity classification system using a computer vision model trained on road images, outputting both pothole localization and a severity class per pothole to prioritize road maintenance. The two scenarios compare detection-only versus severity-aware output. Deliverable: inference demo on held-out images with severity labels overlaid, confusion matrix across severity classes, reflection on annotation subjectivity, dataset bias, and risk of under-prioritizing severe potholes.
consultant_notes: Pothole severity classification is a real, evaluable task but severity labels are sparse in public datasets — the student may need to relabel or use a proxy. The severity-ranking scenario adds meaningful AI value over plain detection. Reflection on annotation disagreement and safety-critical deployment risk is substantive.
annotator_notes: Large amount of manual labour for annotating the severity of potholes in the dataset
column11: a simple classifier so not much technical knowledge gained from this. Could have done more.

history: Tree-LSTM (recursive neural net over parse tree) → Sentiment or NLI task vs flat sequential LSTM baseline
description: The student implements a Tree-LSTM from scratch in PyTorch that encodes syntactic parse trees bottom-up, applied to sentiment analysis (SST) or natural language inference (SNLI), comparing it against a sequential LSTM baseline to test whether hierarchical encoding improves performance on structure-dependent examples. Deliverable: working PyTorch implementation, train/val/test results, tree-rendering of examples, reflection on baseline comparison.
consultant_notes: Tree-LSTM is a well-specified architecture with published reference implementations, making it feasible in 30 days for a student comfortable with PyTorch. Scope risk: parsing pipeline setup (SpaCy or Stanford Parser) adds non-trivial engineering overhead. Conceptually rigorous — requires understanding recursive computation graphs — which is appropriate depth for an intro AI course.
column11: but computationally infeasible and also too much to implement for a 30 day project.

history: Context-aware AAC symbol predictor (LM or retrieval) → Adaptive board reordering vs frequency-baseline with click-count evaluation
description: The student builds a symbol communication board for non-verbal users where a language model reorders suggested symbols based on the user's partial message history, comparing against a frequency-baseline. Deliverable: interactive UI with real-time board reordering, simulation or user study showing message-construction time and click count under both conditions, reflection on accessibility risk and data sparsity.
consultant_notes: Strong user need and technically substantive — combining UI engineering with an NLP/retrieval model and a principled evaluation (click count, message time) hits multiple course objectives. Scope risk: the UI layer plus model plus evaluation adds up; a student who underestimates the UI work may not finish. Ethical reflection on deploying to non-verbal users without real user validation is important and assignment-appropriate.
column11: even though its kind of just an LLM wrapper
